description: Someone passes the bread or stays to help with a difficult task; 'mea' acknowledges what was received. It is the ordinary word of thanks at every scale, while 'lua' (lovely) appreciates the thing or moment itself.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for voiced 'm' and let air pass through the nose into middle-height front 'e'. Part the lips and lower the jaw into open central 'a' for the second syllable. Stress 'me'.
usage_notes: 'mea' can accompany the thanked person's name or address term; it also works alone. Repetition and tone can deepen the thanks without changing its scope.
